name: Type-Based Taint Checking and Inference
summary: Source/sink/propagation security policies encoded into a type-based taint discipline, with inference reducing the annotation burden. Less expressive than arbitrary refinement logic but more operationally deployable: many security policies are exactly 'untrusted input must not reach this sink without a sanitizer.' Taint qualifiers can be inferred across APIs and library stubs.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Practical type-based taint checking and inference (Karimipour, Das, Sridharan, Hassanshahi — ECOOP 2025) is the security-refinement counterpart to general refinement types: source/sink/propagation policies are encoded into a type-based taint discipline, with inference reducing the annotation burden.

Taint is less expressive than arbitrary refinement logic but more operationally deployable: many security policies are exactly "untrusted input must not reach this sink without a sanitizer." The important design point is that taint qualifiers can be inferred across APIs and library stubs, making the checker usable on existing code rather than only greenfield programs.

sources.label: Practical Type-Based Taint Checking and Inference
Practical Type-Based Taint Checking and Inference (ECOOP 2025)

sections.title: Design lesson — narrow-but-inferable beats broad-but-annotation-heavy
sections.content:
Taint checking is a strong example of the engineering trade-off in refinement-style typing: a *narrow* predicate language (taint qualifiers form a small lattice) admits **inference**, which makes the checker deployable on existing codebases. Broader refinement logics (LiquidHaskell-style with arbitrary SMT predicates) need user-written annotations and are harder to retrofit.

For language designers contemplating security-typed APIs, the lesson is that **a small qualifier lattice with good inference is more deployable than a rich predicate language with manual annotation**. Taint qualifiers, sensitivity levels, and capability sets all fit this pattern.
